title: Tool System Design
subtitle: Tool System Design
description: Unified management, registration, discovery, and execution of tools with MCP protocol standardization. The tool system defines the capability boundary of agents.
sub_problems: Tool registration: How to define and register tool schema (name, parameters, return values)
Tool discovery: How agents know which tools are available
Permission control: Which tools require user confirmation before execution
Result formatting: How to format tool return results into LLM-understandable text
MCP protocol: Model Context Protocol standardizes tool interfaces
Pseudo-tool guidance: How to use tools/list mechanism to convey tool usage specifications to LLM
MCP stdout protection: How to prevent logs from corrupting JSON-RPC protocol under stdio transport
Tool result token budget: How to design layered return formats to control token consumption per call
Dual-layer tool architecture: How to serve the same tool for both MCP protocol and internal Agent loop
Dynamic tool set composition: How to selectively activate tool subsets by scenario (implementation/evaluation)
Tool schema and implementation separation: How to evolve tool definitions independently without modifying implementation code
Tool result summarization: How to compress raw tool output into LLM-consumable summary text
Tool call lifecycle: How to track all states from pending→running→success/failed
Tool graceful degradation: How to ensure core tools remain available when dependencies are missing
Tool recommendation: How to precisely recommend the most relevant tool subset for current task in large tool sets
Docstring as schema: How to auto-generate LLM-understandable tool descriptions from code comments
AST dynamic registration: How to register tools from source files without importing modules
Method-level exposure control: How class tools expose only specified methods while hiding internal implementation
Tool parameter validation: How to auto-validate parameter types and constraints before execute
Tool context injection: How to dynamically set tool routing information in multi-channel scenarios
Sub-agent tool isolation: How to implement tool-level permission control through independent registries
best_practices: Tool descriptions must be precise: LLM selects tools based on descriptions; vague descriptions cause misselection
Parameters should have defaults: Reduces the number of parameters LLM needs to decide
Return results should be structured: Facilitates LLM parsing and referencing
Dangerous operations need confirmation: File deletion, network requests require human-in-the-loop
Control tool quantity: Too many tools dilute LLM selection accuracy
MCP Server should be thin wrapper layer: Protocol conversion + HTTP forwarding; business logic sinks to independent services
Use pseudo-tools (__IMPORTANT) in tools/list to convey workflow specifications more reliably than system prompt
Search results return index first, fetch details on demand: Can save 10x token consumption
Return errors as strings rather than throwing exceptions: Lets LLM interpret errors and retry
Sub-agents use independent registry instances rather than runtime filtering: Compile-time isolation is safer than runtime checks
Conditional loading driven by environment variables: Unavailable tools not registered, prevents LLM selecting unavailable tools
Separate thin wrapper from service layer: tools/ only does interface adaptation; logic goes in services/
Truncate tool results before summarization: Prevents oversized output from bloating context window
Use AST to check imports before code execution: ImportGuard intercepts dangerous modules before execution
Use natural language schema rather than JSON Schema: LLM understands docstrings better than JSON Schema
Two-stage recommendation controls tokens: BM25 coarse filtering then LLM fine ranking; avoids full tool set bloating context
Decorator registration keeps code and schema in sync: Schema auto-updates when code changes; no manual maintenance needed
exclusive_tool_commands prevents duplicate operations: Keep only first command of same type; prevents LLM repeated calls
Return errors as strings rather than throwing exceptions: Lets LLM see errors and self-correct
MCP tool namespace isolation: Prefix mcp_{server}_ to avoid conflicts with built-in tools
Tool output truncation: Control context window bloat; ExecTool and WebFetch each have limits

values.claude-mem: Array declaration + handler closure; MCP SDK setRequestHandler registration
values.DeepCode: Dual-layer: FastMCP @mcp.tool decorator + nanobot Tool ABC inheritance registration
values.DeepTutor: Module import + __init__.py unified re-export; no decorator/registry
values.MetaGPT: @register_tool decorator + inspect auto-extract docstring/source to generate schema
values.Nanobot: Tool ABC base class + ToolRegistry dict registry; name index O(1) lookup

values.claude-mem: No grouping; 5 tools flat; CORS limits localhost access

values.DeepTutor: YAML valid_tools whitelist + agent-level tool subset allocation
values.MetaGPT: tags grouping + include_functions method-level filtering + 'Class:method' syntax
values.Nanobot: Code-level isolation: main agent 9 tools; sub-agent 7 tools (no message/spawn/cron)

values.DeerFlow(config): Built-in tools use @tool decorator; MCP tools merged via get_cached_mcp_tools()
values.DeerFlow(MCP): Full support for stdio/SSE/HTTP three transport types

values.claude-mem: Native MCP SDK; StdioServerTransport; thin wrapper delegates to Worker HTTP

values.MetaGPT: No MCP support; uses custom schema format (description+signature+parameters)
values.Nanobot: MCPToolWrapper bridge; supports stdio + HTTP dual transport; lazy-load connections

values.DeerFlow(config): Change YAML use path to switch implementation; requires restart
values.DeerFlow(MCP): mtime cache invalidation auto-detects config changes

values.Nanobot: Supports register/unregister dynamic add/remove; no event notification mechanism

Dual-path: runtime inspect reflection + AST static parsing; docstring as schema

solutions.title: config.yaml → ToolConfig → resolve_variable() reflection loading + tool_groups grouping + SubagentConfig whitelist/blacklist
solutions.description: Declarative config.yaml defines tools as (name/group/use) triplets; ToolConfig (Pydantic) parses then loads via resolve_variable(): importlib.import_module(module_path) + getattr(module, variable_name) + isinstance type validation. Four tool_groups (web/file:read/file:write/bash); get_available_tools(groups=["web"]) filters by group. Community tools follow unified pattern: each tool in src/community/ uses @tool("tool_name") decorator; reads YAML extra parameters via get_app_config().get_tool_config("tool_name").model_extra. SubagentConfig supports tools (whitelist) and disallowed_tools (blacklist; defaults to disallow "task" to prevent recursive delegation). ConfigDict(extra="allow") enables parameter pass-through.
design_philosophy: Declarative config: YAML (name/group/use) triplets; change use path to switch tool implementation without code changes
Reflection loading: resolve_variable() dynamically loads via importlib; zero hard-coded dependencies
Group-based permissions: Four tool_groups implement subagent-level tool permission isolation
Parameter pass-through: ConfigDict(extra="allow") + model_extra auto-forwards YAML extra fields to tools
migration_scenarios: Projects managing many tools declaratively (change YAML not code)
Multi-subagent orchestration requiring different tool permissions per agent (tool_groups + whitelist/blacklist)
Open platforms needing community tool plugin extensibility (src/community/ pattern)

solutions.title: MCP protocol integration: McpServerConfig + get_cached_mcp_tools() mtime caching + three transport protocols
solutions.description: backend/src/mcp/ implements complete MCP integration in ~200 lines across 4 files. McpServerConfig (Pydantic) defines enabled/type/command/args/env/url/headers. build_servers_config() iterates enabled servers to build langchain-mcp-adapters parameters with error isolation (single server failure doesn't affect others). get_mcp_tools() reads latest config from disk each time ensuring Gateway API changes take effect immediately. get_cached_mcp_tools() caches based on file mtime + asyncio.Lock prevents concurrency + ThreadPoolExecutor supports sync environments. Supports stdio/SSE/HTTP three transport protocols. resolve_env_variables() recursively resolves $ENV_VAR. Four-level config file lookup priority: parameter→environment variable→CWD→parent directory.
design_philosophy: Config-as-extension: Edit JSON config to onboard new MCP servers; zero code changes
mtime cache invalidation: Auto-detect config changes based on file modification time; Gateway API changes take effect immediately
Error isolation: Single MCP server failure doesn't affect other servers or built-in tools
Transport abstraction: stdio (local process)/SSE (streaming)/HTTP (standard) unified interface
migration_scenarios: Projects needing zero-code MCP server onboarding (GitHub/filesystem/database etc)
Production environments requiring tool hot-updates (mtime auto-detection)
Hybrid deployments mixing local tools (stdio) and remote tools (SSE/HTTP)

solutions.title: ToolManager MCP multi-server management + tool_blacklist + @with_timeout(1200) + fix_tool_call_arguments()
solutions.description: ToolManager (ToolManagerProtocol) in manager.py unifies multiple MCP servers. server_configs list defines 7 tool servers (tool-python/tool-google-search/tool-jina-scrape/tool-reasoning/tool-reader/tool-vqa/tool-transcribe); each configured via StdioServerParameters. get_all_tool_definitions() retrieves all tool schemas; execute_tool_call(server_name, tool_name, arguments) executes calls. tool_blacklist set disables specific tools. @with_timeout(1200) decorator adds 20-minute timeout protection to tool execution. fix_tool_call_arguments() corrects common LLM parameter errors. Supports persistent browser session browser_session.
design_philosophy: MCP native: Standardizes all tool interfaces via Model Context Protocol; 7 tool servers unified management
Blacklist mechanism: tool_blacklist set dynamically disables specific tools; adjustable at runtime
Timeout protection: @with_timeout(1200) 20-minute hard timeout prevents tool execution hangs
Parameter correction: fix_tool_call_arguments() auto-corrects common LLM parameter format errors; improves tool call success rate
migration_scenarios: Projects needing MCP protocol to unify multiple tool server types
Long-running tool execution (code execution/web scraping) requiring timeout protection
Agent systems where LLM tool call parameters frequently error and need auto-correction

solutions.title: MCP thin wrapper layer with 3-layer workflow tool system
solutions.description: claude-mem implements 5-tool minimal interface via MCP thin wrapper + __IMPORTANT pseudo-tool + 3-Layer Workflow (search→timeline→detail). Strategy pattern search engine supports Chroma/SQLite/Hybrid with auto-fallback.
design_philosophy: Thin wrapper: MCP layer only handles protocol conversion; business logic in independent services
Pseudo-tool guidance: __IMPORTANT in tools/list conveys workflow specifications more reliably than system prompt
3-Layer progressive disclosure: search returns index (~50-100 token/item) → timeline shows context → detail fetches full content
Strategy auto-fallback: Chroma unavailable → SQLite; hybrid search combines both
migration_scenarios: Memory-augmented agents needing progressive disclosure to control token consumption
Multi-backend search requiring graceful degradation when primary backend unavailable
Projects using pseudo-tools to guide LLM workflow rather than relying on system prompt

solutions.title: Dual-layer MCP tool architecture
solutions.description: DeepCode separates external MCP protocol tools from internal Agent tools via FastMCP Server + nanobot ToolRegistry dual-layer architecture. MCPToolDefinitions decouples schema from implementation. Sub-agents achieve tool-level permission isolation through independent registries.
design_philosophy: Dual-layer separation: External MCP protocol layer + internal Agent tool layer; each independently evolved
Schema-implementation decoupling: MCPToolDefinitions allows schema evolution without touching implementation
Sub-agent isolation: Independent registry instances provide compile-time tool permission isolation
migration_scenarios: Projects needing to expose internal tools via MCP protocol without tight coupling
Multi-agent systems requiring different tool permissions per agent
Scenarios where tool schema and implementation evolve at different rates

solutions.title: Academic scenario tool system
solutions.description: Thin wrapper + service layer separation: src/tools/ is thin wrapper; real logic in src/services/. Tool files only do re-export and simple adaptation.
design_philosophy: Layered architecture: Thin wrapper (interface adaptation) separate from service layer (business logic)
Maintainability: Tool changes don't require service layer modifications
Testability: Service layer logic testable independently from tool interface
migration_scenarios: Projects with complex business logic needing clean separation from tool interfaces
Academic/research systems where tool interfaces may change frequently

solutions.title: Decorator-driven tool registration with two-stage recommendation system
solutions.description: MetaGPT uses @register_tool decorator + Google docstring to auto-generate schema. BM25 recall + LLM fine-rank two-stage recommendation. tool_execution_map string mapping dispatches execution.
design_philosophy: Decorator-driven: @register_tool keeps code and schema in sync; schema auto-updates on code changes
Docstring-as-schema: Google docstring format auto-generates LLM-understandable schema
Two-stage recommendation: BM25 coarse filtering (20 candidates) → LLM fine-ranking (Top-5); controls token consumption
String dispatch: tool_execution_map enables dynamic tool execution without hard-coded conditionals
migration_scenarios: Projects with many tools needing efficient recommendation without bloating context
Systems where tool schema must stay synchronized with code changes
Scenarios requiring both recall (BM25) and ranking (LLM) for tool selection

solutions.title: ABC registry tool system
solutions.description: Tool ABC base class defines abstract interface; all tools implement name/description/parameters/execute. ToolRegistry dict registry provides O(1) name lookup. Built-in recursive JSON Schema parameter validation before execute.
design_philosophy: ABC contract: Tool ABC enforces consistent interface across all tools
Registry pattern: ToolRegistry dict enables O(1) tool lookup by name
Built-in validation: Recursive JSON Schema validation auto-called before execute; catches parameter errors early
Extensibility: New tools simply inherit Tool ABC and register
migration_scenarios: Projects needing strict tool interface contracts
Systems with many tools requiring fast lookup and validation
Scenarios where parameter validation must happen before tool execution

solutions.title: Multi-layer tool strategy pipeline
solutions.description: OpenClaw builds multi-layer tool strategy pipeline + plugin Hook lifecycle + tool loop detection three-in-one tool system.
design_philosophy: Strategy pipeline: Multiple tool selection strategies layered; each can filter/rank candidates
Plugin Hooks: Lifecycle events (before_select/after_execute) enable extensibility
Loop detection: Prevents infinite tool call loops through execution history tracking
migration_scenarios: Complex Agent systems needing sophisticated tool selection strategies
Projects requiring plugin-based tool system extensibility
Scenarios where tool call loops must be detected and prevented

solutions.title: BaseTool abstract base class with MCP bidirectional bridge
solutions.description: Three-layer architecture: BaseTool abstract base class + MCP bidirectional bridge + execution layer.
design_philosophy: Abstract base: BaseTool enforces consistent tool interface
Bidirectional bridge: Tools work both as MCP servers and internal Agent tools
Layered execution: Separate execution layer from tool definition
migration_scenarios: Projects needing tools to work both as MCP servers and internal Agent tools
Systems requiring clean separation between tool definition and execution

solutions.title: Unified four-element tool structure patterns
solutions.description: All tools follow unified structure: name + description + parameters (JSON Schema) + required fields.
design_philosophy: Unified structure: Consistent four-element pattern across all tools enables standardization
JSON Schema: Formal parameter specification enables validation and documentation
Consistency: Uniform structure simplifies tool discovery and execution
migration_scenarios: Projects standardizing tool definitions across heterogeneous systems
Scenarios requiring formal tool specification for validation and documentation
